I think using `len(self._subwindows)` is the wrong thing to do here. Here's an example:

Say the screen looks like this with '>' being the cursor.
```
  post 9
  post 10
  post 11
  post 12
  post 13
> post 14
  post 15
```

When you page up, the screen will move up 6 posts.

```
  post 3
  post 4
  post 5
  post 6
  post 7
> post 8
  post 9
```

Now consider what happens if `post 5` is realllly large and takes up 4 times the amount of room on the screen. After paging up, your screen will look like this
```
  post 3
  post 4
  post 5




> post 6
```
This is a big problem because we accidentally skipped right over `post 7` and `post 8`! Paging down will have a similar problem if `nav.inverted == True` because in that case, the page is relative to the bottom of the screen. Does that make sense?

I think I see now. Mapping can be done either with a multi-stream output or using `.map`, and `.map` takes an existing `output` node and makes it a multi-output node.

Can we just make a new output node with the desired streams rather than mutating the existing one?

Making nodes mutable has a lot of widespread implications and opens up the complexity to spiral out of control - for example, downstream hashes would need to be recursively recalculated. (See [some of the benefits of immutable data-structures](http://www.yegor256.com/2014/06/09/objects-should-be-immutable.html)). I'd rather not make such a fundamental change unless absolutely necessary.

broadcast_axis comes from the ONNX's axis attribute in operators that support broadcasting - https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Changelog.md#attributes-103

With OP_SET version 6 broadcasting (1,1) on (4,5) would not be permissible. If we are broadcasting (5,) on (4,5) the broadcast_axis will be equal to 1. On the other hand if we broadcast (4,) on (4,5) broadcast axis will be equal to 0.

ONNX with their OP_SET version 7 are updating the broadcast rules to be aligned with numpy broadcasting rules. When that gets consistently updated in ONNX repo we will also update the translation code in mxnet.

This contains the `run-` prefix which I think is not desirable. Ideally you could do this in one step
```bash
...
--checkpoint_dir "$XDG_DATA_HOME/$(basename -s .sh ${0#run-})" \
...
```
But that doesn't seem to work due to some bash foo I don't have.

So one can do it in two steps.
```bash
XDG_DATA_HOME=...
CHECKPOINT_DIR=$(basename ${0%.*})
CHECKPOINT_DIR=${CHECKPOINT_DIR#run-}
...
   --validation_step 5 \
   --checkpoint_dir "$XDG_DATA_HOME/$CHECKPOINT_DIR" \
...
```

TL;DR: I apologize for unnecessary nitpicking.

In general, a view first asks for the number of children of an invalid index, which is, essentially, the number of root nodes. You don't have a tree, so the number of root nodes equals the number of rows. A view, however, may not know it's not a tree, so it will ask how many children each of those root nodes has. Your answer must be 0.

However, you use your model exclusively with a table view. It cannot show children, so it won't ask about the number of children (as far as I understand). Hence, index will always be invalid.

Lo and behold, although the [documentation still explicitly states]((http://doc.qt.io/qt-5/qabstractitemmodel.html#rowCount) that `rowCount` must return 0 even for table models, documentation for view and models [doesn't do so](http://doc.qt.io/qt-5/model-view-programming.html#dimensions-of-the-model).

IMO it's better to preserve the simpler semantics of passing the unmodified timeout value to requests (which then sets it on the socket) than it is to change it to ensure that we spend no more than `timeout` before returning (which is not a guarantee that the caller can fully rely on anyway, since it's not anything like a real-time system). The scenario of the first few requests returning quickly and the last one hanging (which is necessary to trigger the scenario of significantly missing the timeout deadline before returning) seems far-fetched to me. I agree there is a minor correctness issue, but the two applications of the timeout value really deal with different issues: the one passed to requests and set on the socket takes care of stalled out requests, while the one checked in the retry loop takes care of breaking out of the 503 retry-later mode.

(We may also want to investigate using urllib3's retry support, which was introduced over the course of 2014. Maybe it better deals with this socket/total timeout duality?)

And to answer your questions we don't want to send keepalives before safepoint because shutting down PostgreSQL might not work correctly. Keepalive can be sent when:

1. We have updated our leader lock.

So, afaik, `--no-cache-read` (ie the "global" form of the subsystem option) is applied recursively to all instances of the subsystem below that scope. Or, at least: I know that that is sufficient to disable caching everywhere.

The effect is almost identical to a `global_options` option:
```
# disables caching for all tasks
./pants --no-cache-read compile $t
# disables caching for tasks in compile
./pants compile  --no-cache-read $t
./pants --no-cache-compile-read compile $t
# disables caching for zinc
./pants compile.zinc  --no-cache-read $t
./pants --no-cache-compile-zinc-read compile $t
```

```python
def order_func(key):
    # Makes a tuple to use for ordering.  The tuple is an index into each of the `preference_orders`,
    # followed by the key itself.  This gives us integer sorting if you have a key in one of the
    # `preference_orders`, followed by alphabetical ordering if not.
    order_tuple = [order.index(key) if key in order else len(order) for order in preference_orders]
    return order_tuple + [key]
```

`uint` isn't a standard C or C++ type (even if commonly available); that should be `unsigned int`, or if you're feeling lazy, just `unsigned` (though some people strongly dislike the latter).

The `int32_t` etc. types are more portable, but should still technically be prefixed with `std::` (we should also have a `#include <cstdint>` in here to properly bring them in).

I'm reasonably sure this is an ODR violation. `registered_local_types_cpp()` is located inside an anonymous namespace inside a header which means there's going to be one unique function per translation unit, i.e.:
```
a.cpp -> pybind11::detail::<unique_name_a>::registered_local_types_cpp()
b.cpp -> pybind11::detail::<unique_name_b>::registered_local_types_cpp()
```
On the other hand, `get_type_info()` is not in an anonymous namespace, so per ODR we are promising that there is just one of those for all transaltion units. But because of the call highlighted above, the body of `get_type_info()` will be defined differently in different translalation units, i.e. one will call `<unique_name_a>::registered_local_types_cpp()`, another `<unique_name_b>::`, etc.

A possible solution could be to move the definition of `registered_local_types_cpp` into the `PYBIND11_MODULE` macro (without the anon namespace) which would ensure a single definition per module. Although, I'm not sure if GCC will consider the function-local `static` variable as unique or shared among different modules.

@anirudhacharya generally ops are added as experimental because their use or value is uncertain. for example it could be an op that is only used in 1 framework or an op that potentially could be decomposed into other ops without a perf hit. In this case, the op is supported by multiple frameworks such as TF, PyTorch, mxnet, etc. and can be considered a primitive. So the proper course is to add it as a core op - similar to how the trig ops were added recently.
